article.title: WordPress 7.0 Drops Real-Time Collaboration Twelve Days Before Its May 20 Release as Mullenweg Pulls the Cornerstone Feature
article.summary: WordPress 7.0 ships May 20, 2026, but its long-planned real-time co-editing feature was cut from core on May 8 over robustness concerns.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

WordPress 7.0 is scheduled for final release on May 20, 2026, according to [WordPress.org News](https://wordpress.org/news/2026/05/wordpress-7-0-release-candidate-3/). But the version will ship without real-time collaboration, the simultaneous multi-user editing feature that contributors had spent years building. On May 8, 2026, the WordPress core team confirmed that "Real Time Collaboration will not be included in the 7.0 release and will be re-evaluated during the 7.1 release cycle," as stated in the [WordPress.org News](https://wordpress.org/news/2026/05/wordpress-7-0-release-candidate-3/) Release Candidate 3 announcement.

The decision came from WordPress co-founder Matt Mullenweg. According to the [Make WordPress Core](https://make.wordpress.org/core/2026/05/08/rtc-removed-from-7-0/) post announcing the change, "@matt made the decision to remove real-time collaboration from WordPress 7.0."

## What We Know

Real-time collaboration was Phase 3 of a four-phase roadmap and would have allowed multiple users to edit a post simultaneously and see changes instantly, as reported by [Search Engine Journal](https://www.searchenginejournal.com/wordpress-7-0-will-ship-without-real-time-collaboration/574330/).

The [Make WordPress Core](https://make.wordpress.org/core/2026/05/08/rtc-removed-from-7-0/) post explains that Mullenweg "is not confident the current approach is robust enough to include in Core at this time, citing concerns around surface area, race conditions, server load, memory efficiency, and recurring bugs found through fuzz testing." The post described the move as "a difficult decision, especially given the amount of work that has gone into the feature, but it is being made in service of shipping a stable and reliable WordPress 7.0 release for our users."

In comments reported by [Search Engine Journal](https://www.searchenginejournal.com/wordpress-7-0-will-ship-without-real-time-collaboration/574330/), Mullenweg said "I'm not confident with RTC being in core at this point," citing the same set of technical concerns. He also pointed to external pressure on the project, attributing delays to "the WP Engine lawfare and depositions being a time DoS for critical people," per [Search Engine Journal](https://www.searchenginejournal.com/wordpress-7-0-will-ship-without-real-time-collaboration/574330/).

The removal did not move the ship date. [Search Engine Journal](https://www.searchenginejournal.com/wordpress-7-0-will-ship-without-real-time-collaboration/574330/) reported that "the release schedule remains as is." The [Make WordPress Core](https://make.wordpress.org/core/2026/04/22/wordpress-7-0-release-party-updated-schedule/) schedule still lists a Dry Run and 24-hour code freeze on May 19, 2026, followed by general release on May 20.

The RC3 build that dropped the feature landed on May 8, 2026. Because RTC was pulled, the [WordPress.org News](https://wordpress.org/news/2026/05/wordpress-7-0-release-candidate-3/) announcement notes that "this RC3 version is no longer considered a 'new Beta 1'" and that RC3 marks the hard string freeze point of the cycle. The announcement also cites "143+ issues addressed since RC2." Hosts including Kinsta, Bluehost, GoDaddy, WordPress.com, XServer, and Ionos had helped test variations of the new RTC architecture, according to [WordPress.org News](https://wordpress.org/news/2026/05/wordpress-7-0-release-candidate-3/).

The release timeline had already been extended once. The [Make WordPress Core](https://make.wordpress.org/core/2026/04/22/wordpress-7-0-release-party-updated-schedule/) schedule moved the final release to "May 20th, 2026" to give contributors more time to deliver, in the team's words, "the most stable and most performant it can be, while still delivering the much anticipated cornerstone features."

## What Still Ships in 7.0

With real-time collaboration deferred, the headline platform addition in WordPress 7.0 is the Abilities API. The [WordPress Developer Blog](https://developer.wordpress.org/news/2025/11/introducing-the-wordpress-abilities-api/) describes it as "a first-class, cross-context functional API that other tools and applications can use to interface with WordPress," providing "a central registry where WordPress capabilities (or abilities) are registered in a format that's both machine-readable and human-friendly."

The API is meant to address a long-standing fragmentation problem. As the [WordPress Developer Blog](https://developer.wordpress.org/news/2025/11/introducing-the-wordpress-abilities-api/) puts it, "WordPress doesn't currently force a standard method to build and communicate a public API, so the available options could be anything from a series of action and filter hooks, to publicly accessible global functions, to a series of REST API endpoints." The server-side PHP implementation was approved to be merged in WordPress 6.9, per the [WordPress Developer Blog](https://developer.wordpress.org/news/2025/11/introducing-the-wordpress-abilities-api/).

WordPress 7.0 extends that work to the browser. According to [Make WordPress Core](https://make.wordpress.org/core/2026/03/24/client-side-abilities-api-in-wordpress-7-0/), the release "now provide[s] a counterpart JavaScript API that can be used to implement client-side abilities like navigating, or inserting blocks." The [Make WordPress Core](https://make.wordpress.org/core/2026/03/24/client-side-abilities-api-in-wordpress-7-0/) post says the API "provides a common interface that AI agents, workflow automation tools, and plugins can use to interact with WordPress," and that the client-side work aims to "integrate with browser agents/extensions and WebMCP." It ships as two packages, `@wordpress/abilities` and `@wordpress/core-abilities`, exposing functions such as `registerAbility` and `executeAbility`, as documented by [Make WordPress Core](https://make.wordpress.org/core/2026/03/24/client-side-abilities-api-in-wordpress-7-0/).

## What We Don't Know

The [Make WordPress Core](https://make.wordpress.org/core/2026/05/08/rtc-removed-from-7-0/) post says "a plan will be shared for broader testing and continued iteration to help prepare the feature for a future release," but no firm date for shipping real-time collaboration has been set; the [WordPress.org News](https://wordpress.org/news/2026/05/wordpress-7-0-release-candidate-3/) announcement only commits to re-evaluating it during the 7.1 cycle. The full final feature list for the May 20 build, beyond the Abilities API work, was not detailed in the release announcements reviewed here.
